problem: `read_from_inode` reads `security.capability` through the normal permission-checked xattr API. On ramfs, `get_xattr` checks `MAY_READ`, so a non-root process executing a mode `0111` file with file capabilities gets `EACCES` while the kernel is trying to load exec credentials, even though execute-only privileged binaries must still be executable.
fix: Read `security.capability` through an internal xattr helper that bypasses user read permission for the exec credential calculation, or add an inode/xattr API specifically for kernel-internal security.capability reads and use it here.

problem: The setuid-root-with-file-capabilities exception is applied to the file permitted/inheritable sets but not to the effective bit. A non-root caller executing a setuid-root binary that has a `security.capability` xattr with `permitted=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` and the effective flag clear will still get that capability in its effective set because `exec_euid` is root.
fix: Exclude the setuid-root file-capability exception from the root effective-bit fast path, e.g. compute `let setuid_root_file_caps = has_file_capabilities && !self.ruid().is_root() && exec_euid.is_root();` and only treat root as effective when `!setuid_root_file_caps`.
diff:
453	        // Linux treats root specially when the executable has no file capabilities, or when the
454	        // real UID is root. The setuid-root + file-capability exception is handled by excluding
455	        // the `euid == 0` fast path when a file capability xattr is present.
456	        let grant_root_file_sets =
457	            !no_root && (self.ruid().is_root() || (!has_file_capabilities && exec_euid.is_root()));
...
470	        let file_effective = if (!no_root && exec_euid.is_root())
471	            || file_capabilities.is_some_and(FileCapabilities::has_effective_flag)

problem: `set_fsuid_unchecked` only clears file-related effective capabilities when fsuid changes from root to non-root; it never restores the permitted ones when fsuid changes back to root. A root process with permitted `CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE` that calls `setfsuid(1000)` and then `setfsuid(0)` permanently loses that effective capability until some unrelated UID transition resets the effective set.
fix: Use one shared file-capability mask and add the reverse transition: when `!old_fsuid.is_root() && fsuid.is_root()`, OR `effective_capset` with `permitted_capset() & fsuid_cap_mask`.

problem: Filesystem-UID capability fixups ignore `SECBIT_NO_SETUID_FIXUP`. With that securebit set, changing fsuid from 0 to a nonzero UID should not adjust effective capabilities, but `set_fsuid_unchecked` still clears the file-related capabilities; `set_resuid_unchecked` also calls this before its `no_setuid_fixup` check.
fix: Check `self.securebits().no_setuid_fixup()` before doing any fsuid capability adjustment, and ensure `set_resuid_unchecked` does not call a capability-adjusting fsuid path before honoring that securebit.

problem: `set_keep_capabilities(false)` enforces locked securebits, but exec must always clear `SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS` even when `SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS_LOCKED` is set. The exec path calls this method after switching to the new image, so a process that locked KEEP_CAPS on can make exec fail at the no-return stage instead of successfully clearing only KEEP_CAPS and preserving the lock bit.
fix: Add a separate exec-only helper that stores `self.securebits() - SecureBits::KEEP_CAPS` without rejecting locked KEEP_CAPS, and use that from exec instead of `set_keep_capabilities(false)`.
